---
title: "NTFS Forensics with The Sleuth Kit"
subtitle: "CSCI 4623 — Digital Forensics"
format: html
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
number-sections: false
code-copy: true
---

## Overview

**Image:** `ntfs_lab.img` (created by `setup_ntfs_lab.sh`)

**Tools:** TSK (`fsstat`, `fls`, `istat`, `icat`, `blkstat`, `blkcat`,
`ffind`, `ifind`, `tsk_recover`), `analyzeMFT`

**Assumed knowledge:** FAT and ext filesystem forensics, TSK command
basics (`fls`, `istat`, `icat`), partition analysis with `mmls`.

### Case background

Your team has received a forensic image of workstation WS-042, assigned
to contractor J. Smith (badge C-1847). Smith's access was revoked at
03:00 UTC on 2024-03-11 after network monitoring flagged repeated
connections to 192.168.1.88, a host that does not appear in the corporate
asset inventory and is not associated with any authorised service.
Preliminary evidence suggests that sensitive HR data — including a full
payroll export — was copied to this unregistered internal host in the
two hours preceding revocation.

Your task is to examine the NTFS volume for corroborating evidence:
confirm what files were present, how they were stored, whether any traces
were deliberately concealed, and what the filesystem's own journals
record about activity in that window.

### How NTFS differs from ext and FAT

| Concept | FAT | ext | NTFS |
|---|---|---|---|
| File metadata | Directory entry | Inode | MFT entry |
| Metadata location | In directory | Inode table | `$MFT` file |
| Small file data | Cluster | Block | Resident `$DATA` (in MFT) |
| Named data streams | No | No | Yes (`$DATA:streamname`) |
| Change journal | No | `$LogFile` (limited) | `$UsnJrnl` (full history) |
| Timestamps per file | 2 | 3 | 8 (4 in `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` + 4 in `$FILE_NAME`) |

Every object in NTFS — including the filesystem metadata itself — is a
file with a record in the `$MFT`. System files occupy MFT entries 0–11
and are named with a leading `$`.

---

## Task 0 · Setup and Orientation

Before examining individual files, establish the geometry of the volume:
sector size, cluster size, MFT location, and total addressable range.
These values anchor every subsequent address you will encounter — a
cluster number only means something once you know how large a cluster is
and where they start.

### 0.1 — Identify the image

```bash
file ntfs_lab.img
```

```
ntfs_lab.img: DOS/MBR boot sector, code offset 0x52+2,
  OEM-ID "NTFS    ", sectors/cluster 8, Media descriptor 0xf8,
  heads 255, hidden sectors 63, dos < 4.0 BootSector (0x80),
  FAT (1Y bit by descriptor); NTFS, sectors 262143,
  $MFT start cluster 4, $MFTMirr start cluster 16383,
  bytes/RecordSegment 2^(-1*-10), clusters/index block 1,
  serial number 0wa4b2c3d1e0f5
```

`file` reads the NTFS boot sector (`$Boot`, MFT entry 7) and surfaces
the key layout parameters. Note that this image has no partition table
— it is a bare volume image, acquired starting at the first byte of the
NTFS filesystem rather than at the disk's MBR. This is the common case
when imaging individual volumes rather than whole disks; there are no
partition offsets to account for when passing the image to TSK tools.

### 0.2 — Filesystem statistics

```bash
fsstat ntfs_lab.img
```

```
FILE SYSTEM INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------
File System Type: NTFS
Volume Serial Number: WA4B2C3D1E0F5678
OEM Name: NTFS
Volume Name: WS042-JSMITH
Version: Windows XP

METADATA INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------
First Cluster of MFT: 4
First Cluster of MFT Mirror: 16383
Size of MFT Entries: 1024 bytes
Size of Index Records: 4096 bytes
Range: 0 - 95
Root Directory: 5

CONTENT INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------
Sector Size: 512
Cluster Size: 4096
Total Cluster Range: 0 - 32767
```

Work through the output in two groups. The metadata section tells you
where the MFT lives (cluster 4) and how large each record is (1024
bytes). `Range: 0 - 95` means 96 MFT entries have been allocated on
this volume. The content section tells you the addressable unit for data:
a 4096-byte cluster spanning 8 sectors. Every block address you see in
run lists and `blkstat` output is a cluster number; multiply by 4096 to
get a byte offset into the image.

The volume label `WS042-JSMITH` confirms this is Smith's workstation
image. In ext, the inode table location is a fixed block address recorded
in the superblock. In NTFS, there is no separate inode table — the MFT
is itself a file whose run list (stored in its own `$DATA` attribute)
tells TSK where MFT records are on disk.

---

## Task 1 · NTFS Metadata Files

In ext, `fsstat` points you to a fixed inode table. In NTFS, the
equivalent metadata is distributed across a set of special files, each
with a reserved MFT entry number. Before examining individual user files,
identify these system files and understand what each one contains — they
will surface repeatedly as you trace evidence.

### 1.1 — List the root directory

```bash
fls ntfs_lab.img
```

```
d/d 5-144-4:    .
d/d 5-144-4:    ..
d/- 0-144-1:    $MFT
d/- 1-144-1:    $MFTMirr
d/- 2-144-1:    $LogFile
d/- 3-144-1:    $Volume
d/- 4-144-1:    $AttrDef
d/d 5-144-4:    $OrphanFiles
d/- 6-144-1:    $Bitmap
d/- 7-144-1:    $Boot
d/- 8-144-1:    $BadClus
d/- 9-144-1:    $Secure
d/- 10-144-1:   $UpCase
d/- 11-144-1:   $Extend
d/d 26:         System
d/d 33:         Users
```

The `d/-` prefix on system files means TSK recognises them as allocated
but non-browsable — they are presented as if they were directory entries
in the root, even though most are not directories. Their MFT entry
numbers are fixed by the NTFS specification: `$MFT` is always entry 0,
`$Bitmap` always entry 6, and so on.

### 1.2 — Examine $MFT (entry 0)

```bash
istat ntfs_lab.img 0
```

```
MFT Entry Header Values:
Entry: 0        Sequence: 1
$LogFile Sequence Number: 5765169
Allocated File
Links: 1

$STANDARD_INFORMATION Attribute Values:
Flags: Hidden, System
...

Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 72
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-1)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 80
Type: $DATA (128-2)                   Name: $Data  Non-Resident  size: 98304
  3584 3585 3586 ... (24 clusters)
Type: $BITMAP (176-3)                 Name: N/A   Non-Resident  size: 16
```

The `$DATA` attribute of `$MFT` *is* the MFT — 96 entries × 1024 bytes
= 98,304 bytes, stored in the cluster run beginning at cluster 3584.
The `$BITMAP` attribute tracks which of those 96 entries are currently
allocated. This is worth pausing on: the structure that describes all
files on the volume is itself described as an entry within that same
structure.

### 1.3 — Pull the volume name from $Volume

```bash
istat ntfs_lab.img 3
```

```
Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 72
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-1)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 78
Type: $VOLUME_NAME (96-2)             Name: N/A   Resident   size: 24
Type: $VOLUME_INFORMATION (112-3)     Name: N/A   Resident   size: 12
Type: $DATA (128-4)                   Name: $Data  Resident   size: 0
```

The `$VOLUME_NAME` attribute (type 96) holds the label as a UTF-16LE
string. Extract it with `icat ntfs_lab.img 3-96-2 | strings`. The
`$VOLUME_INFORMATION` attribute records the NTFS version and a flags
field that can indicate a dirty (uncleanly unmounted) volume — relevant
because a dirty flag suggests the system crashed or was powered off
without a clean shutdown, which affects journal integrity.

### 1.4 — System file reference table

| Entry | Name | Forensic relevance |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `$MFT` | Master File Table — source of all metadata |
| 1 | `$MFTMirr` | First 4 MFT entries mirrored for recovery |
| 2 | `$LogFile` | Metadata redo/undo journal |
| 3 | `$Volume` | Volume name, NTFS version, dirty flag |
| 4 | `$AttrDef` | Attribute type definitions |
| 5 | `.` | Root directory |
| 6 | `$Bitmap` | One bit per cluster — allocated vs. free |
| 7 | `$Boot` | Boot sector (first 8 KB of volume) |
| 9 | `$Secure` | Security descriptors |
| 11 | `$Extend` | Directory containing `$UsnJrnl`, `$Quota`, `$ObjId` |

---

## Task 2 · MFT Entry Anatomy

An MFT entry is NTFS's equivalent of an inode, but where an inode is a
fixed-length structure with named fields, an MFT entry is a variable-
length sequence of typed attributes. The attribute type code determines
what the attribute contains; the resident/non-resident flag determines
where the content lives. Learning to read `istat` output as an attribute
list — rather than as a flat set of fields — is the core skill for NTFS
forensics.

### 2.1 — Recursive listing

```bash
fls -r ntfs_lab.img
```

```
d/d 26:         System
d/d 27:         System/Logs
r/r 38-128-1:   Users/jsmith/Documents/incident_summary.txt
r/r 39-128-1:   Users/jsmith/Documents/payroll_export.csv
r/r 40-128-1:   Users/jsmith/Documents/contracts_index.txt
r/r 41-128-1:   Users/jsmith/Desktop/project_notes.txt
r/r 42-128-1:   Users/jsmith/Downloads/VPN_client_setup.exe
r/r 42-128-4:   Users/jsmith/Downloads/VPN_client_setup.exe:Zone.Identifier
r/r 43-128-1:   Users/jsmith/AppData/Roaming/sync_config.ini
r/r 43-128-4:   Users/jsmith/AppData/Roaming/sync_config.ini:credentials
...
```

The inode address format `42-128-4` encodes three values: MFT entry 42,
attribute type 128 (`$DATA`), attribute instance ID 4. Two entries
sharing an MFT number — `42-128-1` and `42-128-4` — belong to the same
MFT record. This is how NTFS Alternate Data Streams appear in TSK output.
You will examine both ADS entries in detail in Task 4.

### 2.2 — Inspect incident_summary.txt

```bash
istat ntfs_lab.img 38
```

```
MFT Entry Header Values:
Entry: 38        Sequence: 1
$LogFile Sequence Number: 6291612
Allocated File
Links: 1

$STANDARD_INFORMATION Attribute Values:
Flags: Archive
Owner ID: 0
Security ID: 256
Created:        2024-03-11 01:55:14 (UTC)
File Modified:  2024-03-11 01:55:14 (UTC)
MFT Modified:   2024-03-11 01:55:14 (UTC)
Accessed:       2024-03-11 01:55:14 (UTC)

$FILE_NAME Attribute Values:
Flags: Archive
Name: incident_summary.txt
Parent MFT Entry: 36   Sequence: 1
Allocated Size: 0   Actual Size: 271

Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 72
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-1)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 96
Type: $DATA (128-2)                   Name: $Data  Resident   size: 271
```

Walk through the attribute list from top to bottom. `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`
(type 16) is always present and always resident; it holds the four
timestamps above, plus the archive/hidden/system flags, owner ID, and
security descriptor index. `$FILE_NAME` (type 48) is also always
resident — it must be, because the directory index B-tree references it
directly when NTFS resolves a path. It carries its own independent copy
of the four timestamps, which become important in Task 7.

Two points to commit to memory. First, the `$FILE_NAME` attribute records
the parent MFT entry (36), which is the `Documents` directory — this is
how NTFS reconstructs paths even for deleted files whose directory entry
has been cleared. Second, the four `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` timestamps are
writable by any process with normal file access; `$FILE_NAME` timestamps
are updated by the NTFS kernel driver during directory index operations
and are significantly harder to manipulate from user space.

### 2.3 — Attribute type reference

| Type code | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 16 (0x10) | `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` | Timestamps, flags, owner/security IDs |
| 48 (0x30) | `$FILE_NAME` | Name, parent entry, second timestamp set |
| 96 (0x60) | `$VOLUME_NAME` | Volume label string |
| 112 (0x70) | `$VOLUME_INFORMATION` | NTFS version, dirty flag |
| 128 (0x80) | `$DATA` | File content (resident or non-resident) |
| 144 (0x90) | `$INDEX_ROOT` | Directory B-tree root node |
| 160 (0xA0) | `$INDEX_ALLOCATION` | Directory B-tree non-resident overflow |
| 176 (0xB0) | `$BITMAP` | Allocation tracking for directories and `$MFT` |

---

## Task 3 · Resident vs. Non-Resident Attributes

When a file's data fits within the available space in its 1024-byte MFT
record, NTFS stores it inline as a *resident* attribute — no cluster
allocation occurs at all. The content lives entirely inside the MFT.
When data is too large, NTFS marks the `$DATA` attribute *non-resident*
and stores a *run list* (an extent map) in its place: a compact encoding
of which clusters hold the content, and in what order.

This distinction matters forensically for two reasons. Resident data
survives as long as the MFT entry survives, even if the surrounding
clusters have been reallocated. Non-resident data can be partially
overwritten cluster by cluster, creating recovery scenarios where some
extents are intact and others are not.

Smith's manager has claimed the payroll export was too large to have been
typed or pasted manually. Confirming its non-resident status — and
locating its cluster run — is the first step in verifying that claim and
establishing that the data was physically present on this volume.

### 3.1 — Resident: incident_summary.txt

From Task 2, `$DATA` is resident at 271 bytes. Confirm the content is
returned directly from the MFT with no cluster lookup:

```bash
icat ntfs_lab.img 38
```

```
INTERNAL — RESTRICTED
Case: 2024-0311
Subject: J. Smith, contractor, badge C-1847
Access revoked: 2024-03-11 03:00 UTC
Summary: Three files confirmed copied to unregistered host 192.168.1.88
between 02:14 and 02:31 UTC on 2024-03-11. No malware identified.
Assigned analyst: review this workstation image for corroborating evidence.
```

The data was returned directly from the MFT record. No cluster lookup
was required. If the surrounding clusters had been reallocated — or if
the file had never been written to clusters at all — `icat` would still
return this content as long as the MFT entry itself was intact.

### 3.2 — Non-resident: payroll_export.csv

```bash
istat ntfs_lab.img 39
```

```
Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 72
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-1)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 98
Type: $DATA (128-2)                   Name: $Data  Non-Resident  size: 3121408
  Start VCN: 0   End VCN: 761
  3072 3073 3074 ... (762 clusters)
```

The `$DATA` attribute is non-resident. Its content field is not the file
data — it is a run list: a sequence of cluster numbers describing where
the data lives on disk. The VCN range (Virtual Cluster Number 0 through
761) tells you the logical extent of the file. The physical cluster run
beginning at 3072 is where those logical clusters are mapped on disk.

### 3.3 — Verify cluster geometry

Before reading from the cluster run, calculate the expected cluster count
independently to confirm the run list is consistent with the recorded
file size:

```bash
python3 -c "import math; print(math.ceil(3121408 / 4096))"
```

```
763
```

The run list reports 762 contiguous clusters — one fewer than the ceiling,
because the last cluster is only partially filled (the remainder is cluster
slack). The numbers are consistent. Now confirm the first cluster of the
run holds what we expect:

```bash
blkcat ntfs_lab.img 3072 | head -c 80 | xxd
```

```
00000000: 456d 706c 6f79 6565 4944 2c46 6972 7374  EmployeeID,First
00000010: 4e61 6d65 2c4c 6173 744e 616d 652c 4465  Name,LastName,De
00000020: 7061 7274 6d65 6e74 2c53 616c 6172 792c  partment,Salary,
00000030: 5353 4e5f 4c61 7374 342c 4261 6e6b 526f  SSN_Last4,BankRo
00000040: 7574 696e 674c 6173 7434 0d0a 4530 3030  utingLast4..E000
```

The cluster begins with the CSV header row, confirming this is the start
of `payroll_export.csv`. The HR data was physically present on this
volume and is directly recoverable from the cluster run.

::: {.callout-warning}
`blkstat ntfs_lab.img 3072` will report "Not Allocated" for this cluster.
This is counterintuitive: the field reflects whether the cluster is listed
as *free* in `$Bitmap`. A cluster in use by a file reads "Not Allocated"
because it is not available for new allocation. A free cluster reads
"Allocated." The naming convention is inverted relative to common intuition.
:::

---

## Task 4 · Alternate Data Streams

A named `$DATA` attribute — called an Alternate Data Stream (ADS) —
allows a single MFT entry to carry multiple independent data payloads.
The filename visible to users and most tools refers only to the default
(unnamed) `$DATA` attribute. Named streams are invisible to `ls`, `cat`,
`wc`, and forensic keyword search passes that iterate over default streams
only. The file size reported by the directory index reflects only the
default stream.

On Windows, the NTFS driver uses a stream named `Zone.Identifier` on
files downloaded from the Internet to record provenance: the download URL,
referring page, and security zone. This is legitimate and forensically
valuable. Malicious use of ADS follows the same mechanism: store a payload
in a named stream on an innocuous-looking file, then access it with a
stream-qualified path.

The image contains two ADS artifacts. One records where `VPN_client_setup.exe`
was downloaded from. The other, on `sync_config.ini`, may explain the
exfiltration mechanism.

### 4.1 — Identify ADS in fls output

```bash
fls -r ntfs_lab.img | grep ":"
```

```
r/r 42-128-4:   Users/jsmith/Downloads/VPN_client_setup.exe:Zone.Identifier
r/r 43-128-4:   Users/jsmith/AppData/Roaming/sync_config.ini:credentials
```

Both entries share their MFT entry number with their parent file,
confirming they are additional `$DATA` attributes in the same MFT record.
Note that nothing in the default `fls` output flags these as unusual —
they appear inline with regular files. A tool that does not parse the
attribute type field would miss them entirely.

### 4.2 — Examine attribute layout with istat

```bash
istat ntfs_lab.img 43
```

```
Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 72
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-1)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 94
Type: $DATA (128-1)                   Name: $Data  Resident   size: 68
Type: $DATA (128-4)                   Name: credentials  Resident   size: 52
```

Two `$DATA` attributes in the same MFT record. Instance 1 is the default
stream (the visible INI content). Instance 4 is the `credentials` stream.
Both are resident — small enough to live inside the 1024-byte MFT record
alongside the other attributes.

### 4.3 — Extract the default stream

```bash
icat ntfs_lab.img 43
```

```
[SyncTool v2.1]
server=backup.internal.corp
interval=3600
compression=true
```

Benign-looking configuration. No indication of exfiltration tooling from
the default stream alone.

### 4.4 — Extract the credentials stream

> **↳ Your task:** Extract the `credentials` named stream using its
> stream-qualified inode address from the `fls` output. What does the
> `remote` field contain, and how does it connect to the network alert?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

```bash
icat ntfs_lab.img 43-128-4
```

```
api_key=sk-4623-EXFIL-a7f3c9d1e2b8
remote=sftp://192.168.1.88/drop
```

The remote SFTP destination matches the IP address in the network
monitoring alert. The API key provides authentication. Both were stored
in a named stream invisible to any tool that reads only the default
stream — the INI file would have appeared benign in a standard file
review.
:::

### 4.5 — Examine the Zone.Identifier stream

> **↳ Your task:** Extract the `Zone.Identifier` stream from
> `VPN_client_setup.exe` (MFT entry 42). What do the `ZoneId`,
> `ReferrerUrl`, and `HostUrl` fields tell you about where this file
> came from? Why is this stream a more reliable provenance indicator
> than the file's SI timestamps?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

```bash
icat ntfs_lab.img 42-128-4
```

```
[ZoneTransfer]
ZoneId=3
ReferrerUrl=http://transfer.sh/upload
HostUrl=http://transfer.sh/VPN_client_setup.exe
```

`ZoneId=3` is the Windows Internet zone — the file was downloaded from
the Internet rather than a local or intranet source. The `HostUrl`
identifies a public file-transfer service, not an internal software
distribution point. The Windows shell writes this stream automatically
at download time through the `IAttachmentExecute` interface; it cannot
be set retroactively through normal file operations. This makes it
substantially harder to fabricate after the fact than SI timestamps,
which are writable by any process.
:::

---

## Task 5 · Deletion and Recovery

When a file is deleted in NTFS, the MFT entry is marked unallocated and
the clusters belonging to its `$DATA` attribute are cleared in `$Bitmap`.
The MFT record itself is not zeroed — the `$FILE_NAME`, `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`,
and (for resident files) `$DATA` attributes remain physically in place
until the entry is reused by a new file. For non-resident files, the
data clusters are preserved until reallocated.

TSK exploits this by scanning the MFT for entries where the allocated
flag is cleared, exposing deleted files that the filesystem no longer
acknowledges. The recovery window closes as new file activity reuses MFT
entries and clusters. On a live system under active use this window can
close within minutes; on a forensic image taken shortly after the incident,
recently deleted files are typically still recoverable.

The image contains four deleted files in different states. Your task is
to find them, recover what you can, and assess the recovery status of
each.

### 5.1 — List unallocated entries

```bash
fls -r -d ntfs_lab.img
```

```
r/r * 62-128-1: System/Logs/access.log
r/r * 71-128-1: Users/jsmith/Documents/final_export.csv
r/r * 74-128-1: Users/jsmith/Desktop/staging.txt
r/r * 75-128-1: Users/jsmith/Desktop/draft_note.txt
```

The `*` marks each entry as unallocated. All four show a reconstructed
path — TSK derives this from the `$FILE_NAME` attribute's parent MFT
entry field, not from a live directory index. Even though the directory
no longer lists these files, the MFT records still point to their last
known parent.

### 5.2 — Recover with tsk_recover

```bash
mkdir -p recovered
tsk_recover ntfs_lab.img recovered/
cat recovered/access.log
```

```
2024-03-11 02:14:03 WS-042 user=jsmith src=192.168.1.88 action=READ  file=payroll_export.csv
2024-03-11 02:19:45 WS-042 user=jsmith src=192.168.1.88 action=COPY  file=contracts_q1.docx
2024-03-11 02:28:07 WS-042 user=jsmith src=192.168.1.88 action=READ  file=keys.pem
2024-03-11 02:31:12 WS-042 user=jsmith src=192.168.1.88 action=COPY  file=payroll_export.csv
```

The log records two copy operations on `payroll_export.csv` from the
workstation to `192.168.1.88`, consistent with the network alert. The
log was deleted — presumably to conceal this activity — but the MFT
entry was still unallocated at acquisition time, and the content was
recovered intact.

### 5.3 — Check recovery status for a resident deleted file

Not all deleted files are equally recoverable. `draft_note.txt` is small
enough to have been stored resident. Check whether it has a cluster run:

> **↳ Your task:** Run `istat ntfs_lab.img 75`. Does `$DATA` have a
> cluster run? What does that mean for carving-based recovery if the
> MFT entry had been reused?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

```bash
istat ntfs_lab.img 75
```

```
Attributes:
Type: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (16-0)    Name: N/A   Resident   size: 72
Type: $FILE_NAME (48-1)               Name: N/A   Resident   size: 88
Type: $DATA (128-2)                   Name: $Data  Resident   size: 6
```

`$DATA` is resident — no cluster run exists. The file's content was
never written to a data cluster. A carving tool scanning unallocated
clusters for file signatures would never find this file. Recovery
depends entirely on the MFT entry surviving intact. Once that entry is
reused for a new file, the content is gone.
:::

### 5.4 — Trace a renamed and deleted file with ffind

`final_export.csv` was renamed from `temp_export.csv` before deletion.
The MFT record holds only the last-known filename — the one active at
deletion time.

> **↳ Your task:** Run `ffind ntfs_lab.img 71`. What does the `*` prefix
> indicate? Why does the original name `temp_export.csv` not appear here,
> and where would you look to find evidence of the rename?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

```bash
ffind ntfs_lab.img 71
```

```
* /Users/jsmith/Documents/final_export.csv
```

The `*` indicates the name was recovered from an unallocated MFT entry,
not from a live directory index. The parent directory no longer has an
index entry for MFT 71 — it was removed at deletion time. The filename
comes from the `$FILE_NAME` attribute surviving in the unallocated record.

The rename updated the `$FILE_NAME` attribute in place, so only the final
name survives in the MFT entry. The original name `temp_export.csv` is
gone from the MFT — but the rename event is recorded in `$UsnJrnl`,
which you will examine in Task 6.
:::

---

## Task 6 · Journaling and MFT Dump

NTFS maintains two journals. `$LogFile` (entry 2) is a redo/undo log for
metadata operations — its purpose is crash consistency, not investigative
history. It wraps quickly and its binary format is not designed for
forensic consumption.

`$UsnJrnl` (Update Sequence Number journal) is different. It records
every filesystem change — creation, deletion, rename, attribute modification,
data write — as a sequential stream of fixed-size records. It does not
store file content, only change events tagged with the filename and MFT
entry number of the affected file. On a production system it wraps after
a configurable size (default 32 MB on modern Windows), but the history
it preserves can span days or weeks.

The `$UsnJrnl` is where you will find the rename from `temp_export.csv`
to `final_export.csv` that `ffind` could not show.

### 6.1 — Locate $UsnJrnl under $Extend

```bash
fls ntfs_lab.img 11
```

```
r/r 24-128-1:   $UsnJrnl
r/r 25-128-1:   $ObjId
```

```bash
istat ntfs_lab.img 24
```

```
Attributes:
Type: $DATA (128-1)    Name: $Max   Resident   size: 32
Type: $DATA (128-2)    Name: $J     Non-Resident  size: 8192
  Run list: 7680 7681 (2 clusters)
```

`$UsnJrnl` carries two named streams: `$Max` (journal configuration)
and `$J` (the change records). Both are named `$DATA` attributes in the
same MFT entry — the identical ADS mechanism from Task 4, used here by
the operating system itself for its own housekeeping.

### 6.2 — Extract and parse the $J stream

```bash
icat ntfs_lab.img 24-128-2 > usn.bin
xxd usn.bin | head -40
```

```
00000000: 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000  ................
...
00001000: 6000 0000 0000 0000 2800 0000 0000 0000  `.......(.......
00001010: 4700 0000 0100 0000 6800 0000 0000 0000  G.......h.......
00001020: 0200 0000 0200 0000 0000 8000 2000 0000  ............ ...
00001030: 1a00 7400 6500 6d00 7000 5f00 6500 7800  ..t.e.m.p._.e.x
00001040: 7000 6f00 7200 7400 2e00 6300 7300 7600  p.o.r.t...c.s.v.
```

The leading zeros are the journal's sparse region before the first
record. The first USN record begins at offset `0x1000`. The 4-byte
record length at that offset is `0x60` = 96 bytes. At offset
`0x30` within the record you can read the filename in UTF-16LE:
`t.e.m.p._.e.x.p.o.r.t...c.s.v.` — `temp_export.csv`.

Parse all records to extract the event sequence:

```bash
python3 - << 'EOF'
import struct

REASONS = {
    0x00000001: "DATA_OVERWRITE",  0x00000002: "DATA_EXTEND",
    0x00000100: "FILE_CREATE",     0x00000200: "FILE_DELETE",
    0x00001000: "RENAME_OLD_NAME", 0x00002000: "RENAME_NEW_NAME",
    0x00004000: "INDEXABLE_CHANGE",0x80000000: "CLOSE",
}

data = open("usn.bin", "rb").read()
i = 0
while i < len(data) - 60:
    rec_len = struct.unpack_from("<I", data, i)[0]
    if rec_len < 60 or rec_len > 65536:
        i += 8
        continue
    reason  = struct.unpack_from("<I", data, i + 40)[0]
    fn_len  = struct.unpack_from("<H", data, i + 52)[0]
    fn_off  = struct.unpack_from("<H", data, i + 54)[0]
    name    = data[i+fn_off : i+fn_off+fn_len].decode("utf-16-le", errors="replace")
    reasons = "|".join(v for k,v in REASONS.items() if reason & k)
    print(f"  {reasons:<30} {name}")
    i += rec_len
EOF
```

```
  FILE_CREATE                    temp_export.csv
  DATA_EXTEND|CLOSE              temp_export.csv
  RENAME_OLD_NAME                temp_export.csv
  RENAME_NEW_NAME                final_export.csv
  FILE_DELETE|CLOSE              final_export.csv
  FILE_CREATE                    payroll_export.csv
  ...
```

The journal has preserved the full sequence: `temp_export.csv` was
created, written, renamed to `final_export.csv`, then deleted. The
`RENAME_OLD_NAME` / `RENAME_NEW_NAME` pair is the event that the MFT
entry alone could not show. The journal is a sequential audit trail that
persists across deletion and MFT entry reuse, as long as the journal
clusters themselves have not been overwritten.

### 6.3 — Parse $MFT with analyzeMFT

`analyzeMFT` parses the raw `$MFT` bytes into a CSV with one row per
entry, including deleted and unallocated entries, with all attribute
fields decoded into readable columns.

> **↳ Your task:** Extract `$MFT`, run `analyzeMFT`, and check the
> output for the four deleted entries from Task 5. What does the `In Use`
> column show, and how do the timestamps compare to what `istat`
> reported?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

```bash
icat ntfs_lab.img 0 > mft.raw
analyzeMFT -f mft.raw -o mft.csv
grep -i "access.log\|final_export\|staging\|draft_note" mft.csv
```

The `In Use` column reads `False` for all four deleted entries —
confirming they are flagged as unallocated in the MFT bitmap despite
their records being physically present. Timestamps match `istat` output.
`analyzeMFT` adds value by presenting the entire MFT in a single tabular
view, making cross-entry timeline analysis practical without running
`istat` on each entry individually.

If `analyzeMFT` is not installed: `pip install analyzeMFT --break-system-packages`
:::

---

## Task 7 · Mini-Challenge — Timestomping

Anti-forensic timestomping exploits the fact that `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`
timestamps are writable by any process through normal Windows API calls —
a single call to `SetFileTime` can set them to any arbitrary value. The
`$FILE_NAME` timestamps, by contrast, are written by the NTFS kernel
driver when a directory index entry is created or updated. They do not
respond to user-space timestamp modification APIs.

When SI and FN timestamps diverge — especially when SI timestamps are
implausibly old while FN timestamps are consistent with surrounding files
— the divergence is a strong indicator of deliberate manipulation. This
is detectable with `istat` alone: it reports both sets in every MFT entry.

There are two timestomped files in the image. They are not equally
obvious — the second requires comparing individual timestamp fields
rather than spotting an obviously wrong year. Find both, and for each
document: the filename, the MFT entry number, which specific timestamps
diverge, the SI value, the FN value, and what forensic conclusion you
would draw about what the attacker was trying to accomplish.

::: {.callout-tip}
Run `istat` on each regular user file and compare the
`$STANDARD_INFORMATION` and `$FILE_NAME` blocks side by side. The two
anomalies have different signatures — one is a wholesale replacement of
all four SI timestamps, the other is a targeted modification of a single
field.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### Answer

**File 1: project_notes.txt (MFT entry 41)**

```bash
istat ntfs_lab.img 41
```

```
$STANDARD_INFORMATION:
Created:       2001-01-01 00:00:00 (UTC)
File Modified: 2001-01-01 00:00:00 (UTC)
MFT Modified:  2001-01-01 00:00:00 (UTC)
Accessed:      2001-01-01 00:00:00 (UTC)

$FILE_NAME:
Created:       2024-03-11 01:57:22 (UTC)
File Modified: 2024-03-11 01:57:22 (UTC)
MFT Modified:  2024-03-11 01:57:26 (UTC)
Accessed:      2024-03-11 01:57:22 (UTC)
```

All four SI timestamps were replaced with 2001-01-01 — a round-number
date with no plausible legitimate explanation on a 2024 workstation. The
FN timestamps record the actual creation time. In a naive timeline
analysis relying only on SI timestamps, this file would appear to predate
the workstation itself, placing it outside the incident window.

**File 2: contracts_index.txt (MFT entry 40)**

```bash
istat ntfs_lab.img 40
```

```
$STANDARD_INFORMATION:
Created:       2024-03-11 01:56:44 (UTC)
File Modified: 2015-06-15 09:00:00 (UTC)
MFT Modified:  2024-03-11 01:56:44 (UTC)
Accessed:      2024-03-11 01:56:44 (UTC)

$FILE_NAME:
Created:       2024-03-11 01:56:44 (UTC)
File Modified: 2024-03-11 01:56:44 (UTC)
MFT Modified:  2024-03-11 01:56:44 (UTC)
Accessed:      2024-03-11 01:56:44 (UTC)
```

Only the SI `File Modified` timestamp was altered — to 2015-06-15.
Created, MFT Modified, and Accessed are consistent with the FN values
and with the creation time of surrounding files. This is a targeted
stomp: only the Modified timestamp was changed, moving the file's
"last modified" marker in a timeline nine years into the past, potentially
outside the investigation window. The FN Modified timestamp of
2024-03-11 directly contradicts the SI value and exposes the manipulation.
:::

---

## Summary

| Task | Concept | Key commands |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | NTFS geometry, bare volume images | `fsstat`, `file` |
| 1 | System metadata files, `$Volume` attributes | `fls`, `istat 0`, `istat 3` |
| 2 | MFT entry anatomy, attribute types | `istat`, `fls -r` |
| 3 | Resident vs. non-resident, run list geometry | `istat`, `blkstat`, `blkcat` |
| 4 | Alternate Data Streams, `Zone.Identifier` | `fls`, `icat <entry>-128-<id>` |
| 5 | Deletion, recovery states, rename tracing | `fls -r -d`, `tsk_recover`, `ffind` |
| 6 | `$UsnJrnl` structure and parsing, MFT dump | `icat 24-128-2`, `analyzeMFT` |
| 7 | SI vs. FN timestamp divergence, timestomping | `istat` (compare both blocks) |

### Reference: NTFS system file MFT entries

| Entry | Name | Role |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `$MFT` | Master File Table |
| 1 | `$MFTMirr` | MFT mirror (first 4 entries) |
| 2 | `$LogFile` | Metadata redo/undo journal |
| 3 | `$Volume` | Volume name, version, dirty flag |
| 4 | `$AttrDef` | Attribute type definitions |
| 5 | `.` | Root directory |
| 6 | `$Bitmap` | Cluster allocation map |
| 7 | `$Boot` | Boot sector |
| 8 | `$BadClus` | Bad cluster list |
| 9 | `$Secure` | Security descriptors |
| 10 | `$UpCase` | Unicode uppercase table |
| 11 | `$Extend` | Extension directory (`$UsnJrnl`, `$Quota`, `$ObjId`) |
